description: A Ukrainian news website reports that its site has been under constant cyberattack during the Russian-Ukrainian armed conflict. The DDOS attack incapacitated their systems and they had to find alternative means to publish the news by posting shortened stories on Facebook, Twitter, and LinkedIn. | The websites of several Ukrainian banks and government departments, including the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Ministry of Defense, Ministry of Internal Affairs, Security Service (SBU) and Cabinet of Ministers became inaccessible following a large DDoS attack. Most other sites came online within two hours of the attack but latency and outages continued into the following day for others. | More than 600 websites belonging to the defence ministry in Kyiv and other institutions suffered attacks with the launch of thousands of exploits with attempts pointed to at least 20 distinct vulnerabilities. The campaign started mid-February and peaked on 23 February. The attacks sought to infiltrate targets ranging from border defence forces to the national bank and railway authority. They were designed to steal data and explore ways to shut down or disrupt vital defence and civilian infrastructure. The Times, allegedly quoting a source at the SBU, claimed the campaign was co-ordinated by the Chinese government. The SBU went on to deny The Times report. | ... and 7 more unique descriptions

description: A Ukrainian cybersecurity researcher published the biggest leak ever of files and data from Conti following Conti's publicly supporting Russia's invasion of Ukraine. Conti is a syndicate of Russian and Eastern Europe cybercriminals wanted by the FBI for conducting attacks on hundreds of US organizations and causing millions of dollars in losses. The thousands of internal documents and communications leaked include evidence that appears to suggest Conti operatives have contacts within the Russian government, including the FSB intelligence service. | Russian government entities and state-owned companies have been targeted over events in Ukraine, with the websites of the Kremlin, flagship carrier and major lender among those to have seen outages or temporary access issues.

The data was uploaded on February 27. | A Russian TV channel in France was allegedly compromised by Anonymous.

description: The developer behind the popular "node-ipc" NPM package released a new tampered version to condemn Russia's invasion of Ukraine. The alterations introduced by its maintainer RIAEvangelist brought about undesirable behavior by targeting users with IP addresses located either in Russia or Belarus, and wiping arbitrary file contents and replacing them with a heart emoji. In a latter release, any time the node-ipc module functionality gets called, it prints to STDOUT a message taken out of the peacenotwar module, as well as places a file on the user's Desktop directory with contents relating to the current war-time situation of Russia and Ukraine.

description: The Sayanmoloko Semyonishna dairy plant in Khakassia, Russia, experienced a ransomware attack in December 2024 attributed to a LockBit variant. The attack encrypted labeling and tracking systems, hijacked printers to output anti-war leaflets, and disabled the company website while milk processing continued. Operations were restored within several days.

target_actor: At least one individual in Ukraine
description: The article reports researchers observed a months-long phishing/credential-harvesting operation targeting users of UKR.NET, a popular Ukrainian webmail and news service. The campaign ran from June 2024 through April 2025 and was attributed to Russian state-backed BlueDelta (APT28/Fancy Bear/Forest Blizzard). Researchers said the actors created multiple fake UKR.NET login pages and sent phishing emails with PDF attachments containing embedded links to the fraudulent portals, with more than 20 linked PDF lure files identified. The purpose was assessed as harvesting credentials and gathering intelligence supporting broader Russian objectives; the reporting did not quantify how many users were successfully compromised.

description: For at least two years spanning from January 2020 through February 2022, Russian state-sponsored threat actors targeted American cleared defense contractors (CDCs) to acquire and exfiltrate sensitive data and export-controlled technology. These CDCs were contracted by the Department of Defense (DoD) and the U.S. Intelligence Community in areas including command and control, intelligence, surveillance, and weapon development. Threat actors used brute force techniques to collect credentials and sent spear-phishing emails embedded with malicious links.?

description: Russian threat actor APT 29 targeted diplomatic entities with spear- phishing emails disguised as embassy administrative notices, which were sent from compromised email addresses of other diplomatic entities. description: Germany’s main opposition party, the Christian Democratic Union, suffered a cyberattack just ahead of European Parliamentary elections. Germany’s interior ministry did not disclose the extend of the attack or the suspected perpetrator, but acknowledged it was “serious.” The attack occurred shortly after Germany’s Social Democratic party was attacked by Russian hackers. The party briefly took down parts of its IT service as a precaution.

The company did not disclose further details on the source code access or breached internal systems.

description: The Australian government identified and sanctioned Aleksandr Ermakov as the Russian hacker who breached Medibank, the country’s largest private health insurance provider, in 2022. He stole information from 9.7 million current and former Medibank customers. This is the first time Australia has issued cyber sanctions against an individual since the framework was established in 2021. The U.S. and UK also sanctioned Ermakov.

description: Russian agents hacked residential webcams in Kyiv to gather information on the city’s air defense systems before launching a missile attack on Kyiv. Hackers changed the cameras’ angles to gather information on nearby critical infrastructure facilities and stream the footage on YouTube. Ukraine has since ordered webcam operators in the country to stop live broadcasts.

description: The Ukraine's National Cyber Security Coordination Center (NCSCC) reveals that Russian state-sponsored hackers from APT29 have targeted embassies and international organizations exploiting the WinRAR CVE-2023-38831 vulnerability.
description_merged: The Ukraine's National Cyber Security Coordination Center (NCSCC) disclosed that Russian state-sponsored hackers from APT29, associated with the SVR (COZYBEAR), conducted an exploitive campaign targeting embassies and international organizations. The operation, reported on November 13, 2023, exploited the WinRAR vulnerability CVE-2023-38831, a security flaw in the widely-used file archiving utility, to gain initial access to systems at diplomatic missions and multilateral institutional networks. The NCSCC revelation highlighted a sustained Russian state-sponsored cyber espionage effort directed by the foreign intelligence service's cyber element against diplomatic and international organizational targets, leveraging a commonly used software application as the infection vector to compromise sensitive networks.
description_merged_final: The Ukraine's National Cyber Security Coordination Center (NCSCC) reveals that Russian state-sponsored hackers from APT29 have targeted embassies and international organizations exploiting the WinRAR CVE-2023-38831 vulnerability.

description_merged: On August 8, 2023, the pro-Russia hacktivist collective NoName057(16) claimed responsibility for a series of disruptive DDoS cyberattacks targeting government and public services websites across multiple allied nations, specifically in France and the Netherlands. In France, the collective's targets included the country's customs service, specifically the Direction générale des douanes et droits indirects, as well as the French financial regulator, disrupting key financial oversight and customs operations. In the Netherlands, the targeted websites included the Dutch public transport information website, the local bank SNS, the Groningen seaport authority, and the official website of the municipality of Vlardingen, affecting transportation, banking, port infrastructure, and local government services. These attacks reflect a coordinated anti-NATO DDoS campaign disrupting public-facing government and essential civilian services.

description: Anonymous Russia claims to have conducted a DDoS attack against the website of the Kazakhstan government.
targets_all: Government of Kazakhstan; Ukrainian taxi companies
description_merged: On October 24, 2022, the hacktivist collective Anonymous Russia claimed responsibility for conducting multiple disruptive DDoS operations against geographically disparate targets in the same operational period. The collective specifically reported carrying out a DDoS attack directed against the official website of the Kazakhstan government, striking a Central Asian government entity. Additionally, the group claimed responsibility for a separate DDoS campaign that targeted the websites of six different Ukrainian taxi companies, affecting civilian transportation service providers across Ukraine. This dual-target disruptive campaign by Anonymous Russia struck both a national government website and multiple private-sector civilian transportation platforms, reflecting a broad targeting strategy across different sectors and countries within the same timeframe.
description_merged_final: Anonymous Russia claims to have conducted a DDoS attack against the website of the Kazakhstan government.

description: Anonymous Sudan also claims responsibility for another DDoS attack that targeted Stripe's dashboard for managing business payments, refunds, and operations.
targets_all: Stripe; EFTPS.gov (U.S. Treasury Department's Electronic Federal Tax Payment System)
description_merged: On June 30, 2023, the hacktivist group Anonymous Sudan claimed responsibility for conducting a DDoS attack that specifically targeted Stripe's operational dashboard, a platform that businesses rely on for managing payments, processing refunds, and handling overall transactional operations. In a separate but contemporaneous development, the U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) issued a public warning about ongoing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks affecting organizations across multiple industry sectors throughout the United States. These events targeted financial payment processing infrastructure and, according to the CISA advisory, indicated a broader sustained campaign of disruptive DDoS activity against American entities spanning a range of industry sectors beyond just financial services.
description_merged_final: Anonymous Sudan also claims responsibility for another DDoS attack that targeted Stripe's dashboard for managing business payments, refunds, and operations.
